title: A Theory of Hypoellipticity and Unique Ergodicity for Semilinear Stochastic PDEs
overall_summary: Hairer and Mattingly develop an asymptotic strong Feller and Malliavin-calculus framework for semilinear parabolic SPDEs with polynomial nonlinearities and finite-dimensional additive noise. The main analytic mechanism is to transfer infinitesimal variations in the initial condition to variations in the driving Wiener path, using approximate inverses of the Malliavin covariance matrix and high-mode dissipation to obtain gradient estimates for the Markov semigroup. The paper proves abstract ergodic criteria: asymptotic strong Feller plus a weak topological irreducibility or accessibility condition implies at most one invariant probability measure. It also proves quantitative lower bounds on finite-dimensional projections of the Malliavin matrix under an infinite-dimensional Hörmander-type bracket condition. For the original problem, the most directly portable material is the abstract uniqueness route via asymptotic strong Feller or strong Feller plus support/accessibility, while the detailed finite-dimensional-noise Hörmander machinery is less directly applicable but provides useful templates for smoothing estimates and controllability-based uniqueness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.1, quoted from HM06: gradient criterion for asymptotic strong Feller
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be a Markov semigroup on a Hilbert space H. Let t_n be a nondecreasing sequence of positive times and let δ_n be a positive sequence converging to 0. Suppose that for every bounded Frechet differentiable function φ:H→R with finite sup norm ||φ||∞ and finite derivative sup norm ||Dφ||∞, one has ||D P_{t_n} φ(u)|| ≤ C(||u||)(||φ||∞ + δ_n ||Dφ||∞) for all n and all u in H, where C:R_+→R is a fixed nondecreasing function. Then P_t is asymptotically strong Feller in the sense of Hairer and Mattingly.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result from HM06 rather than reproving it. The idea is that the displayed gradient estimate says that at large times P_{t_n}φ is uniformly continuous at a scale that increasingly ignores the initial Lipschitz constant of φ. One tests P_{t_n} against functions that are Lipschitz for a sequence of metrics approaching the discrete or total-variation metric. The term δ_n ||Dφ||∞ tends to zero, while the ||φ||∞ term remains controlled locally by C(||u||), yielding asymptotic continuity of transition probabilities in the corresponding Wasserstein metrics. This is exactly the asymptotic strong Feller property used later to force disjointness of supports of distinct ergodic invariant measures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2, quoted from HM06: asymptotic strong Feller separates ergodic supports
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be a Markov semigroup on a Polish space X. If P_t is asymptotically strong Feller and μ and ν are two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures for P_t, then supp(μ) ∩ supp(ν) is empty. Here supp(μ) is the topological support: x belongs to supp(μ) if every open ball around x has positive μ-mass.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem from HM06. The underlying argument is that two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures are mutually singular by the ergodic decomposition or Birkhoff ergodic theorem. If their supports shared a point, then the asymptotic strong Feller property would imply that transition probabilities started from nearby points cannot remain asymptotically separated in total variation-like metrics. Since the shared support point can be approached from sets of positive μ- and ν-mass and both measures are invariant, the transition probabilities would force a nontrivial overlap between μ and ν, contradicting mutual singularity. Thus asymptotic smoothing converts topological overlap of supports into measure-theoretic non-singularity, impossible for two distinct ergodic invariant measures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.4: Feller plus weak topological irreducibility plus asymptotic strong Feller gives uniqueness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be a Markov semigroup on a Polish space, assumed Feller in the sense that P_t maps bounded continuous functions to bounded continuous functions. Say P_t is weakly topologically irreducible if for every pair u_1,u_2 in the state space there exists v such that for every open set A containing v there are times t_1,t_2>0 with P_{t_i}(u_i,A)>0 for i=1,2. If P_t is Feller, weakly topologically irreducible, and asymptotically strong Feller, then P_t admits at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume by contradiction that there are two distinct invariant probability measures. By ergodic decomposition it is enough to take two distinct ergodic invariant measures μ_1 and μ_2. Choose u_i in supp(μ_i). Weak topological irreducibility gives a point v such that every neighborhood of v can be hit from both u_1 and u_2 at some positive times. The Feller property upgrades positivity at u_i to positivity uniformly on a small neighborhood B_δ(u_i): for each small ball B_ε(v), there are δ,t_i>0 with inf_{z in B_δ(u_i)} P_{t_i}(z,B_ε(v))>0. Since u_i is in the support of μ_i, μ_i(B_δ(u_i))>0. Invariance gives μ_i(B_ε(v)) = ∫ P_{t_i}(z,B_ε(v)) μ_i(dz) ≥ μ_i(B_δ(u_i)) inf_{z in B_δ(u_i)} P_{t_i}(z,B_ε(v)) > 0. Because ε is arbitrary, v belongs to both supports. This contradicts Theorem 1.2, which says distinct ergodic invariant measures for an asymptotic strong Feller semigroup have disjoint supports.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1: quantitative separation of supports from a gradient estimate
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be a Markov semigroup on a separable Hilbert space H. Assume that the gradient estimate of Proposition 1.1 holds for some nondecreasing function C and sequences t_n, δ_n. If μ_1 and μ_2 are two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures for P_t, then for every u_1 in supp(μ_1) and u_2 in supp(μ_2), one has ||u_1-u_2|| ≥ 1 / C(||u_1||∨||u_2||), where ∨ denotes maximum.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof refines Theorem 1.2 using the quantitative estimate. The paper introduces a sequence of Wasserstein-1 distances d_n induced by metrics d_n on H that converge to the total variation distance on probability measures, and uses the gradient estimate to obtain a bound of the form d_n(P_{t_n}^*δ_{u_1},P_{t_n}^*δ_{u_2}) ≤ ||u_1-u_2|| C(||u_1||∨||u_2||)(1+small_n). Suppose there are support points u_1,u_2 closer than the reciprocal of C. Then choose a set A containing both points with positive μ_i-mass and such that sup_{v_1,v_2 in A} ||v_1-v_2|| C(||u_1||∨||u_2||)<1. Invariance and convexity of the Wasserstein distance imply d_n(μ_1,μ_2) ≤ 1 - α(1 - β(1+small_n)), where α=min(μ_1(A),μ_2(A))>0 and β<1. Letting n→∞ yields total variation distance strictly less than 1. But distinct ergodic invariant measures are mutually singular, hence have total variation distance 1. This contradiction proves the separation bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.2: uniqueness from quantitative asymptotic strong Feller and bounded approximate accessibility
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be a Markov semigroup on a separable Hilbert space H satisfying the gradient estimate used in Theorem 2.1, with function C. Suppose that for every R_0>0 there exist R>0 and T>0 such that for every ε>0 there is a point v with ||v||≤R and P_T(u,B_ε(v))>0 for every u with ||u||≤R_0. Then P_t has at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume two distinct ergodic invariant measures exist. Choose R_0 large enough that the open ball of radius R_0 intersects both supports. The accessibility hypothesis gives, for every ε, a point v of norm at most R and a common time T such that the ε-ball around v can be reached with positive probability from all points in the R_0-ball, hence in particular from support points of both measures. Arguing as in Corollary 1.4, invariance forces the supports of both invariant measures to intersect B_ε(v). Since ||v|| is uniformly bounded in ε, taking ε small produces support points of the two invariant measures at a distance smaller than the quantitative lower bound of Theorem 2.1. This contradiction rules out two invariant probability measures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Assumption A.1 and Proposition 3.4: analytic-polynomial SPDE setup and local mild solutions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper considers SPDEs in a Hilbert space H of the form du = -L u dt + N(u) dt + Σ_{k=1}^d g_k dW_k(t), u_0 in H, where W_k are independent real Brownian motions. Assumption A.1 requires: L:D(L)→H is selfadjoint and satisfies <u,Lu>≥||u||^2; H_α are the associated interpolation spaces; for some a in [0,1), γ_* and β_* greater than -a with γ_*+β_*>-1, the nonlinearity N is a polynomial map N in Poly_n(H_{γ+a},H_γ) for every γ in [-a,γ_*); for every β in [-a,β_*) there is γ in [0,γ_*+1) such that DN^*(u) extends continuously from H_γ into L(H_{β+a},H_β); and g_k belongs to H_{γ_*+1}. Under this assumption, for every u_0 in H there is a stopping time τ>0 such that the SPDE has a unique adapted mild solution on [0,τ), satisfying u_t = e^{-Lt}u_0 + ∫_0^t e^{-L(t-s)}N(u_s) ds + ∫_0^t e^{-L(t-s)}G dW(s), where Gv=Σ v_k g_k. The solution lies almost surely in C([0,τ),H).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a fixed-point argument on C([0,T],H). For a deterministic path ξ, define Φ_{T,ξ}(u_0,u)_t = e^{-Lt}u_0 + ξ(t) + ∫_0^t e^{-L(t-s)}N(u_s) ds. Since N maps H locally Lipschitzly into H_{-a} and the analytic semigroup satisfies ||e^{-Lt}||_{H_{-a}→H} ≤ C t^{-a} with a<1, the integral map is locally Lipschitz with a small factor T^{1-a}. For T small enough, depending on the radius of a ball around e^{-Lt}u_0+ξ(t), Φ is a contraction. Taking ξ to be the stochastic convolution W_L(0,t)=∫_0^t e^{-L(t-s)}G dW(s) gives existence and uniqueness pathwise for small T. Iterating until the norm blows up gives a maximal local solution; the maximal time is a stopping time because the construction depends only on the noise up to that time.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 3.6 and 3.7: parabolic smoothing estimates for the solution and Jacobian
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the analytic-polynomial framework of Assumption A.1, let u_t solve the mild equation and let W_L(s,t)=∫_s^t e^{-L(t-r)}G dW(r). For every T>0 and γ in [0,γ_*+1), Proposition 3.6 gives exponents p_γ≥1 and q_γ≥0 and a constant C such that for 0<t≤T∧τ, ||u_t||_γ ≤ C t^{-q_γ}(1 + sup_{s in [t/2,t]}||u_s|| + sup_{t/2≤s<r≤t}||W_L(s,r)||_γ)^{p_γ}. The Jacobian J_{s,t}, defined by ∂_t J_{s,t}ϕ = -L J_{s,t}ϕ + DN(u_t)J_{s,t}ϕ and J_{s,s}ϕ=ϕ, satisfies Proposition 3.7: for every γ in (0,γ_*+1) there are γ_0<γ, an exponent q̃_γ≥0, and C such that ||J_{t,t+s}ϕ||_γ ≤ C s^{-γ} sup_{r in [s/2,s]}(1+||u_{t+r}||_{γ_0})^{q̃_γ} ||J_{t,t+r}ϕ|| for every ϕ in H and t,s>0. If γ<1-a, one can choose γ_0=0 and q̃_γ=n-1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Both estimates are obtained by bootstrapping regularity using the smoothing of e^{-Lt}. For u_t, one first notes the bound at γ=0. Assuming a bound at regularity γ, write the mild equation from time αt to time t: u_t=e^{-(1-α)tL}u_{αt}+∫_{αt}^t e^{-L(t-s)}N(u_s)ds+W_L(αt,t). Since N maps H_γ to H_{γ-a} and e^{-Lr} gains δ derivatives at cost r^{-δ}, choosing δ<1-a makes the singularity integrable and yields a bound at γ+δ. Iterating reaches any γ<γ_*+1. For the Jacobian, the same variation-of-constants argument is applied to the linearized equation; DN(u) is controlled polynomially by lower regularity norms of u, and the semigroup supplies the gain in derivatives.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.10: backward adjoint linearization equals the adjoint Jacobian
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J_{s,t} be the Jacobian of the solution flow for the SPDE, solving ∂_t J_{s,t}ϕ = -LJ_{s,t}ϕ + DN(u_t)J_{s,t}ϕ. Define K_{s,t} by the backward equation ∂_s K_{s,t}ψ = L K_{s,t}ψ - DN^*(u_s)K_{s,t}ψ, with K_{t,t}ψ=ψ. Under Assumption A.1 and for 0≤s<t, K_{s,t}=J_{s,t}^* as operators on H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For smooth test vectors ϕ,ψ in H_∞, consider f(r)=<J_{s,r}ϕ,K_{r,t}ψ> for r in [s,t]. The regularity estimates ensure that f is differentiable in r and that all pairings are legitimate. Differentiating and using the equations for J and K gives ∂_r f(r)=<(-L+DN(u_r))J_{s,r}ϕ,K_{r,t}ψ> - <J_{s,r}ϕ,(-L+DN^*(u_r))K_{r,t}ψ>=0, with signs arranged according to the backward equation. Hence f is constant. Evaluating at r=s and r=t gives <ϕ,K_{s,t}ψ>=<J_{s,t}ϕ,ψ>, proving K_{s,t}=J_{s,t}^*. Density extends the identity to H.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.11: higher variations of the solution flow
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J^{(n)}_{s,t} denote the n-th derivative of the solution flow with respect to initial data, evaluated on an n-tuple ϕ=(ϕ_1,...,ϕ_n), with possibly different starting times s=(s_1,...,s_n). It solves a linearized equation whose inhomogeneous term is a sum over partitions of {1,...,n} involving higher derivatives D^{(ν)}N(u) applied to lower variations. If β_*>a-1, then there exists γ<γ_*+1 such that for every n>0 there are exponents N_n and M_n with ||J^{(n)}_{s,t}ϕ|| ≤ C sup_{r in [0,t]}(1+||u_r||_γ)^{N_n} sup_{0≤r<v≤t}(1+||J_{r,v}||)^{M_n}, uniformly over n-tuples with ||ϕ_k||≤1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on n. The case n=1 is the ordinary Jacobian. The equation for J^{(n)} is written using the variation-of-constants formula J^{(n)}_{s,t}ϕ = ∫_0^t J_{r,t} G^{(n)}_{s,r}(u_r,ϕ) dr, where G^{(n)} is a finite sum over partitions involving D^{(ν)}N and lower variations. The induction hypothesis bounds all lower variations by powers of sup ||u_r||_γ and sup ||J||. Assumption A.1 controls D^{(ν)}N as an operator into H_{-a}; duality with K=J^* and Proposition 3.9 control the integral of ||J_{r,t}||_{H_{-a}→H}. The condition β_*>a-1 ensures enough regularity for this dual estimate. This yields the stated polynomial bound in solution and Jacobian norms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1 and Corollary 4.3: Malliavin derivative and derivative with respect to the noise
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix u in H and t>0, and let Φ_t^u:C([0,t],R^d)→H be the Itô map sending a Brownian path W to the solution u_t. Under Assumption A.1 and assuming global existence up to t, Φ_t^u is Frechet differentiable. Its derivative in the direction v in C([0,t],R^d) solves d(DΦ_t^u v) = -L(DΦ_t^u v)dt + DN(u_t)DΦ_t^u v dt + G dv(t) in the mild sense. In particular, if v is absolutely continuous with derivative h in L^2([0,∞),R^d), then D_h Φ_t^u = ∫_0^t J_{s,t}G h(s) ds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Itô map is the fixed point of the same contraction map used for local existence. This map is Frechet differentiable jointly in the path and the solution variable, so the implicit function theorem gives differentiability of the fixed point with respect to the driving path on sufficiently short intervals; iteration gives the result for arbitrary t before explosion. The derivative equation follows by differentiating the mild equation. For absolutely continuous path variations, Duhamel's formula for the resulting linear inhomogeneous equation gives D_h u_t = ∫_0^t J_{s,t}G h(s)ds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 4.4 and identity (4.11): Malliavin covariance matrix
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the finite-dimensional additive-noise SPDE, define A_t:L^2([0,∞),R^d)→H by A_t h = ∫_0^t J_{s,t}G h(s) ds. The Malliavin covariance matrix M_t:H→H is M_t=A_t A_t^*, equivalently M_tϕ = Σ_{k=1}^d ∫_0^t <J_{s,t}g_k,ϕ> J_{s,t}g_k ds. Using the backward adjoint K_{s,t}=J_{s,t}^*, one has for every ϕ in H the identity <M_tϕ,ϕ> = Σ_{k=1}^d ∫_0^t <J_{s,t}g_k,ϕ>^2 ds = Σ_{k=1}^d ∫_0^t <g_k,K_{s,t}ϕ>^2 ds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first expression is immediate from A_t h=∫J_{s,t}G h(s)ds and the definition M_t=A_tA_t^*. The adjoint A_t^* maps ξ in H to the L^2 path s↦G^*J_{s,t}^*ξ for s≤t and 0 for s>t. Expanding GG^* in the basis determined by the forced directions g_k yields the integral formula. Proposition 3.10 identifies J_{s,t}^* with K_{s,t}, giving the final representation. This representation is central because it converts lower bounds on the Malliavin matrix into lower bounds on the time-history of the backward adjoint tested against forced directions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.5: regularizing and compactness properties of the Malliavin covariance
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume Assumption A.1 and global existence. For every T>0 and every γ in [0,(1-a)∧1/2), M_T extends almost surely to a bounded linear operator from H_{-γ} to H_γ. More precisely, sup_{ϕ,ψ in H_{-γ}, ||ϕ||_{-γ}=||ψ||_{-γ}=1} <M_Tϕ,ψ> ≤ T C sup_{0≤s<t≤T} sup_k (1+||u_t||)^{2n-2} ||J_{s,t}g_k||^2, where n is the degree of the polynomial nonlinearity and C is deterministic. If L has compact resolvent, then M_T is almost surely compact as an operator on H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Starting from M_T=Σ_k∫_0^T J_{s,T}g_k ⊗ J_{s,T}g_k ds, the bilinear form on H_{-γ} is bounded by Σ_k∫_0^T ||J_{s,T}g_k||_γ^2 ds. Proposition 3.7 controls ||J_{s,T}g_k||_γ by a singular factor (T-s)^{-γ} times polynomial functions of u and low-norm Jacobian bounds. The restriction γ<1/2 makes the square singularity integrable near s=T. If L has compact resolvent, the embedding H_γ into H is compact for γ>0, so the bounded map H_{-γ}→H_γ induces a compact operator on H.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 5.8 and 5.9: strongly dissipative case gives asymptotic smoothing without Malliavin control
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume a Lyapunov structure E exp(V(u_1)) ≤ exp(η' V(u_0)+C_L) with η' in [0,1), and a Jacobian moment bound E||J_{s,t}||^{p_bar} ≤ exp(p_bar η V(u_0)+p_bar C_J) for 0≤s≤t≤1, with p_bar η<1-η'. Let κ=η/(1-η') and C_T=C_J+κ C_L. Then for p in [0,p_bar/2], E||J_{0,n}||^p ≤ exp(pκV(u_0)+p C_T n). Consequently, for any differentiable test function φ, ||D(P_n φ)(u)|| ≤ γ^n e^{κV(u)} sqrt(P_n ||Dφ||^2(u)), where γ=e^{C_T}. If C_T<0, then the semigroup is asymptotically strong Feller.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Jacobian cocycle gives ||J_{0,n}||≤||J_{n-1,n}|| ||J_{0,n-1}||. Conditioning on the sigma-field at time n-1 and applying Hölder separates the Jacobian factor from the Lyapunov factor exp(pκV(u_n)). The Lyapunov inequality is chosen so that κ=η/(1-η') exactly compensates the growth in V when iterated. Repeating this one-step estimate yields the exponential bound on E||J_{0,n}||^p. For the semigroup, D(P_nφ)(u)ξ=E[(Dφ)(u_n)J_{0,n}ξ]. Cauchy-Schwarz and the Jacobian estimate give the displayed gradient bound. If γ<1, this has the form of Proposition 1.1 with no ||φ||∞ term needed, hence implies asymptotic strong Feller.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.14 and Corollary 5.15: regularized inverse of the Malliavin matrix on an unstable subspace
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Π be an orthogonal projection on H and M be a selfadjoint positive operator on H. Fix δ in (0,1] and suppose that for some γ>0, inf_{ξ: ||Πξ||≥δ||ξ||} <Mξ,ξ>/||ξ||^2 ≥ γ. For β>0 set R=1-M(β+M)^{-1}=β(β+M)^{-1}. Then ||ΠR||≤δ∨sqrt(β/γ). In the random setting, if M and γ are random and β is chosen so that P(β≥δ^2γ)≤Cδ^p, then E||ΠR||^p≤(1+C)δ^p. In particular, under the paper's Malliavin lower bound assumption, choosing β_k = δ^3/(U(u_k) C(δ,p_bar)^{1/p_bar}) gives E(||ΠR_k^{β_k}||^p | F_k)≤2δ^p for p≤p_bar.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Rξ has small Π-component, namely Rξ is outside the cone {||Πζ||≥δ||ζ||}, then ||ΠRξ||≤δ||Rξ||≤δ||ξ||. If Rξ lies in the cone, the assumed lower bound gives γ||Rξ||^2≤<MRξ,Rξ>. Since M and β commute through the functional calculus defining R, <(M+β)Rξ,Rξ>=β<ξ,Rξ>≤β||ξ||||Rξ||. Thus ||Rξ||/||ξ||≤sqrt(β/γ), and the same bound controls ||ΠRξ||/||ξ||. The random corollary splits the expectation over the event β≤δ^2γ, where the deterministic lemma gives ||ΠR||≤δ, and its complement, where the operator norm is at most 1 and the complement has probability O(δ^p).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.5: main infinite-dimensional smoothing estimate
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the semilinear SPDE du=-Lu dt+N(u)dt+G dW(t) in the analytic-polynomial framework of Assumption A.1, with global solutions, Jacobian J, second variation J^{(2)}, and Malliavin covariance M_t. Assume: B.1, there is a function U:H→[1,∞), an orthogonal projection Π, and q_bar≥2 such that for every α>0 and p≥1, P(inf_{||Πϕ||≥α||ϕ||} <M_1ϕ,ϕ>/||ϕ||^2 ≤ ε) ≤ C(α,p) U(u_0)^p ε^p for ε≤1, and E U(u_n)^{q_bar}≤C_U^{q_bar} U(u_0)^{q_bar}; B.2, E exp(V(u_1))≤exp(η'V(u_0)+C_L) for η' in [0,1); B.3, for some p_bar≥10 and η>0 with p_bar η<1-η' and 2/q_bar+10/p_bar≤1, E||J_{s,t}||^{p_bar}≤exp(p_bar ηV(u_0)+p_bar C_J) and E||J^{(2)}_{s,t}||^{p_bar}≤exp(p_bar ηV(u_0)+p_bar C_J^{(2)}) for 0≤s≤t≤1; B.4, E||J_{0,1}Π^⊥||^{p_bar}≤exp(p_bar ηV(u_0)-p_bar C_Π), with C_Π-C_J>2κC_L and κ=η/(1-η'). Then for every ζ in [0,(C_Π-C_J)/2-κC_L) there are positive constants C and γ=e^{-ζ} such that for all n in N and all measurable φ:H→R, ||D(P_{2n}φ)(u)|| ≤ e^{4κV(u)}( C U(u)^2 sqrt(P_{2n}φ^2(u)) + γ^{2n} sqrt(P_{2n}||Dφ||^2(u)) ). In particular this type of estimate implies asymptotic strong Feller when applied to bounded differentiable φ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof constructs, for each initial direction ξ, a noise variation h^ξ that approximately cancels the effect of D_ξu_t. Malliavin integration by parts gives D_ξP_tφ(u)=E φ(u_t)∫_0^t h_s^ξ dW_s + E(Dφ)(u_t)ρ_t, where ρ_t=D_ξu_t-D_{h^ξ}u_t. The control h is chosen blockwise: on even intervals [2k,2k+1], h=A_k^*(M_k+β_k)^{-1}J_kρ_{2k}; on odd intervals it is zero. Then ρ_{2k+2}=J_{2k+1}R_k^{β_k}J_{2k}ρ_{2k}, where R_k^{β}=β(M_k+β)^{-1}. Decomposing R into Π and Π^⊥ components, B.4 contracts the Π^⊥ part while Lemma 5.14 and B.1 make the Π part small with high probability by a suitable adapted choice of β_k depending on U(u_k). This yields exponential decay of E||ρ_{2n}||^p with a Lyapunov weight. The stochastic integral ∫h dW is a Skorokhod integral because h is generally not adapted inside each block, but it is block-adapted. The paper uses the Skorokhod isometry, bounds on Malliavin derivatives of J and A from the second variation J^{(2)}, and the U-moment assumption to obtain a uniform bound on E|∫_0^{2n}h dW|^2 by C e^{(8η+2κ)V(u)}U(u)^2||ξ||^2. Combining this bound with Cauchy-Schwarz for the two terms in the integration-by-parts identity yields the displayed estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.7 and Theorem 6.12: Hörmander-type lower bound for the Malliavin matrix
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the SPDE du=-Lu dt+N(u)dt+G dW(t) satisfying Assumption A.1 and the moment assumption C.1: there is Ψ_0:H→[1,∞) such that for each T in (0,T_0] and p≥1, E sup_{T≤t≤2T}||u_t||^p≤CΨ_0(u_0)^p and E sup_{T≤s<t≤2T}||J_{s,t}||^p≤CΨ_0(u_0)^p. Fix γ in [-a,γ_*) and β in [-a,β_*) with γ+β≥-1, and define Poly(γ,β)=Poly(H_γ,H_{-β-1})∩Poly(H_{γ+1},H_{-β}). Let F=-L+N. Starting from A_1={g_1,...,g_d}, define increasing sets A_i of polynomial vector fields by adding, for admissible Q in A_i and all multiindices α,σ, the Taylor-bracket fields Q_α and [F_σ,Q_α]. For N, set <ϕ,Q_N(u)ϕ>=Σ_{Q in A_N}<ϕ,Q(u)>^2. Let Π be a finite-rank orthogonal projection and S_α={ϕ≠0:||Πϕ||≥α||ϕ||}. Assume C.2: for every α>0 there exist N and Λ_α:H→[0,∞) such that inf_{ϕ in S_α}<ϕ,Q_N(u)ϕ>/||Πϕ||^2≥Λ_α(u)^2 and E Λ_α(u_t)^{-p}≤CΨ(u_0)^p for all p≥1 and t>0. Then there exists θ>0 such that for every α in (0,1), p≥1, and t>0, P(inf_{ϕ in S_α}<M_tϕ,ϕ>/||ϕ||^2≤ε)≤C Ψ(u_0)^{θp} ε^p for ε≤1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is organized as Theorem 6.12, an equivalent negligible-events statement. Work backward from a fixed terminal time T using K_{s,T}=J_{s,T}^*. The base step uses <M_Tϕ,ϕ>=Σ_k∫<g_k,K_{s,T}ϕ>^2ds: if this is small, then each process t↦<g_k,K_{t,T}ϕ> is small uniformly on a shortened interval I_δ=[T/2,T-δ], except on a negligible event controlled by Hölder estimates on K. The induction step proves that smallness of <Q(u_t),K_{t,T}ϕ> implies smallness for the bracket-generated fields. To do this, write u_t=v_t+G W(t), where v_t has better time regularity, and expand Q(u_t) as a finite Wiener polynomial in W with coefficient processes <Q_α(v_t),K_{t,T}ϕ>. The Wiener-polynomial estimate of Theorem 7.1 says smallness of the polynomial forces smallness of all coefficient processes unless a coefficient oscillates too fast; Proposition 6.2 excludes the fast-oscillation alternative with high probability. Differentiating <Q_α(v_t),K_{t,T}ϕ> in time and using the equations for v and K produces commutators [Q_α,F_σ], and a second expansion returns from v_t to u_t. Iterating reaches all Q in A_N. A separate lemma shows that if ϕ belongs to S_α, then K_{T-δ,T}ϕ still belongs to S_{cα} and has a comparable Π-component with high probability. The nondegeneracy assumption C.2 then says the bracket quadratic form cannot be small unless Λ_α^{-1} is very large, an event controlled by its inverse-moment hypothesis. Choosing δ=ε^r with r sufficiently small balances the singular estimates and completes the Malliavin lower bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.1: lower bounds for Wiener polynomials with non-adapted coefficients
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let W_1,...,W_d be independent standard Brownian motions on [0,1]. For a multiindex α=(α_1,...,α_l), write W_α(t)=W_{α_1}(t)...W_{α_l}(t), with W_empty=1. Let A_α(t) be arbitrary stochastic processes, not necessarily adapted, such that A_α=0 whenever |α|>m. Define Z_A(t)=Σ_α A_α(t)W_α(t). Then there is a universal family of negligible events Osc_W^m, depending only on m, such that outside Osc_W^m, the implication ||Z_A||_{L∞([0,1])}≤ε ⇒ either sup_α||A_α||_{L∞}≤ε^{3^{-m}} or sup_α||A_α||_{Lip}≥ε^{-3^{-(m+1)}} holds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the degree m. The case m=0 is trivial. For m≥1, partition [0,1] into intervals of length ε^κ. On each interval, expand W_α(t) around the left endpoint using Brownian increments. The first-order increment terms form a degree m-1 Wiener polynomial whose coefficients are the shifted families M_jA; all higher-order increments and coefficient variations are error terms. Brownian Hölder and supremum estimates, outside universal negligible events, make these errors smaller than the assumed ε-size of Z_A, provided κ and related exponents are chosen appropriately. A separate elementary lemma says that a nontrivial linear combination of Brownian increments cannot remain uniformly too small on every interval, uniformly over all coefficient vectors. Hence smallness of Z_A forces smallness of each degree m-1 polynomial Z_{M_jA}. Applying the induction hypothesis to those lower-degree polynomials gives smallness of all nonconstant coefficients A_α. Finally, the original identity Z_A=A_empty+Σ_{α≠empty}A_αW_α and Brownian supremum bounds give smallness of A_empty. The alternative throughout is that some coefficient has a very large Lipschitz norm, yielding the second outcome.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.3: dense families give finite-dimensional nondegeneracy
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a separable Hilbert space and let {g_i}_{i≥1} be a collection in H whose linear span is dense in H. Define symmetric nonnegative forms Q_n by <h,Q_n h>=Σ_{i=1}^n <g_i,h>^2. If Π:H→H is an orthogonal projection onto a finite-dimensional subspace, then there exists N such that for every α>0 there is c_α>0 with <h,Q_n h>≥c_α||Πh||^2 for every h satisfying ||Πh||≥α||h|| and every n≥N.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume the conclusion fails. Then for some α there are h_n with ||Πh_n||=1, ||h_n||≤α^{-1}, and <h_n,Q_nh_n>→0. By weak compactness of bounded sets in H, pass to a subsequence h_n converging weakly to h. Since Π has finite rank, Πh_n→Πh strongly, so ||Πh||=1. For each fixed k, weak continuity of h↦<g_k,h> gives <g_k,h>=lim_n<g_k,h_n>=0, because <h_n,Q_nh_n> controls the first k coefficients for n≥k. Thus h is orthogonal to the dense span of the g_i, so h=0, contradicting ||Πh||=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.1: general-purpose asymptotic strong Feller theorem for polynomial semilinear SPDEs
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider du=-Lu dt+N(u)dt+Σ_{k=1}^d g_k dW_k(t) under Assumption A.1, with N having top homogeneous multilinear part N_m. Assume D.1: L has compact resolvent; there is a measurable V:H→R_+ with V(u)≥c||u||^α and E exp(V(u_1))≤C exp(η'V(u_0)) for η'<1; and for every p>0 and δ>0 there is C such that sup_{t≤1}E||u_t||^p, E sup_{s,t≤1}||J_{s,t}||^p, and sup_{s,t≤1}E||J^{(2)}_{s,t}||^p are all bounded by C exp(δV(u_0)). Assume D.2: g_k in H_∞ and, defining A_0={g_1,...,g_d} and A_{k+1}=A_k∪{N_m(h_1,...,h_m):h_j in A_k}, the linear span of A_∞=∪_k A_k is dense in H. Then there exist C,κ>0 and γ in (0,1) such that ||D(P_{2n}φ)(u)|| ≤ C e^{κV(u)}( sqrt(P_{2n}φ^2(u)) + γ^n sqrt(P_{2n}||Dφ||^2(u)) ) for every integer n>0. Hence P_t is asymptotically strong Feller. If additionally β_*>a-1, then for every finite-dimensional linear map T:H→R^m, the projected transition probabilities T_*P_2(u,·) have C^∞ densities with respect to Lebesgue measure on R^m.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: D.1 supplies the Lyapunov, Jacobian, and second-variation bounds required for Theorem 5.5. D.2 and Lemma 8.3 imply the Hörmander nondegeneracy assumption C.2 for every finite-rank projection Π, because repeated Lie brackets with constant forced vector fields recover the fields N_m(h_1,...,h_m). Theorem 6.7 therefore supplies the Malliavin lower bound B.1. Since L has compact resolvent, projecting away from sufficiently many low eigenmodes makes e^{-Lt}Π^⊥ arbitrarily small. Variation-of-constants for J_{0,1}Π^⊥ and the moment bounds in D.1 then give Assumption B.4 with C_Π as large as needed. Applying Theorem 5.5 yields the gradient estimate and asymptotic strong Feller property. For smooth densities of finite-dimensional projections, the Malliavin lower bound gives inverse moments of the projected covariance matrix, while Proposition 3.11 gives Malliavin derivatives of all orders; the standard Malliavin criterion quoted from Nualart then yields C^∞ densities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.18: asymptotic strong Feller for a class of stochastic reaction-diffusion equations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let D be a compact smooth manifold, a smooth bounded domain, or a hypercube in dimension m≤3, and consider du=Δu dt + f∘u dt + Σ_{i=1}^d g_i dW_i(t), where u(t):D→R^l, the forcing functions g_i are smooth enough, and f:R^l→R^l is a polynomial of odd degree n≥3. Assume the top homogeneous part f_n satisfies <f_n(u,...,u,v),v><0 for every nonzero u,v in R^l. Define A_tilde_1={g_i} and A_tilde_{k+1}=A_tilde_k∪{F_n(h_1,...,h_n):h_j in A_tilde_k}, where F_n is the leading n-linear reaction term acting pointwise. If the linear span of A_tilde_∞ is dense in H^2, then for every ζ>0 there is C such that for all u in H^2 and all φ:H^2→R, ||D P_t φ(u)||_{L^2→R} ≤ C( ||φ||_{L∞} + e^{-ζt} sup_{v in H^2}||Dφ(v)||_{H^2→R} ). In particular P_t is asymptotically strong Feller in H^2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The dissipativity of the leading polynomial gives pointwise and L∞ absorbing estimates after subtracting the stochastic convolution. Bootstrapping with the heat semigroup yields H^2 bounds on solutions, Jacobians, and second variations; after a positive time these bounds are uniform in the initial condition. The bracket-density assumption and Lemma 8.3 imply the finite-dimensional Malliavin lower bound with U=1 via Theorem 6.7. High modes are made contractive by choosing Π to project onto sufficiently many low Laplacian modes, giving B.4. Theorem 5.5 then gives an H^2-gradient estimate with a Lyapunov prefactor. A final smoothing estimate for J_{0,2}:L^2→H^2, uniform in the initial condition, removes the prefactor and yields the displayed bound in the L^2 initial direction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.21 and Proposition 8.25: unique ergodicity for stochastic real Ginzburg-Landau on the circle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider du(x,t)=ν∂_x^2u(x,t)dt + ηu(x,t)dt - u(x,t)^3dt + Σ_{j=1}^d g_j(x)dW_j(t) on the circle S^1, with ν,η>0 and smooth real functions g_j. Suppose: first, some linear combination g of the g_j has only finitely many simple zeroes; second, the smallest vector space containing all g_j and closed under the trilinear operation (f,g,h)↦fgh is dense in H^1(S^1). Then the equation has exactly one invariant probability measure. Proposition 8.25 supplies the accessibility input: under the first condition, there is K>0 such that for every ε>0 there are v with ||v||_{H^1}≤K and T>0 such that P_T(u_0,B_ε(v))>0 for all u_0 in H^1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence of an invariant measure is standard. The density of the trilinear algebra is precisely the bracket-density condition for the cubic reaction, so Theorem 8.18 gives the asymptotic strong Feller estimate in H^1. For accessibility, the paper constructs deterministic controls in the forced directions. Take a large control f(x,t)=ε^{-γ}g(x) on a time interval and zero otherwise. Choose v_0 solving the elliptic equation νv_0''+ηv_0-v_0^3+ε^{-γ}g=0, which exists as a critical point of a coercive energy. Although v_0 can grow as ε→0, the uncontrolled dissipative evolution for one unit of time sends it into a uniformly bounded H^1 set; call the result v. Because g has only simple zeroes, v_0 is large on most of the circle, and an estimate shows that the controlled solution is driven close in L^2 to v_0, then parabolic smoothing upgrades closeness after the final unforced step to H^1 closeness to v. The support theorem for additive Brownian forcing implies positive probability of following this deterministic controlled path. With asymptotic strong Feller and this bounded approximate accessibility, Corollary 2.2 yields uniqueness.

proof_techniques.name: Asymptotic strong Feller plus support/accessibility uniqueness
proof_techniques.description: The paper separates the proof of uniqueness of invariant measures into a smoothing part and a topological part. Smoothing is encoded by the asymptotic strong Feller property or by a quantitative gradient estimate; topology is encoded by weak irreducibility or by existence of a bounded approximately accessible point. The key abstract fact is that asymptotic strong Feller semigroups cannot have two distinct ergodic invariant measures whose supports overlap, so any condition forcing overlap of supports yields uniqueness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the SPDE with Dirac drift, one possible route is to prove strong Feller or asymptotic strong Feller for P_t, then show every invariant measure has a common support point or that all bounded sets can reach a common small ball with positive probability. This abstract part does not depend on the polynomial finite-dimensional-noise framework.

proof_techniques.name: Gradient estimate as a usable ASF criterion
proof_techniques.description: Rather than proving asymptotic strong Feller directly from transition probabilities, the paper uses estimates of the form ||D P_{t_n}φ(u)|| ≤ C(u)(||φ||∞+δ_n||Dφ||∞), with δ_n→0. Such estimates show that the semigroup eventually smooths bounded observables up to a vanishing dependence on their initial Lipschitz constant. This is particularly useful because the estimate can be derived from linearized dynamics and Malliavin integration by parts.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can obtain a Bismut-Elworthy-Li type formula or an approximation-uniform gradient estimate for the regularized drifts G_{1/n}, this criterion provides a direct path to uniqueness via the paper's abstract ergodic corollaries.

proof_techniques.name: Transfer of variation from initial condition to noise
proof_techniques.description: The central smoothing proof writes D_ξP_tφ as a sum of two terms by choosing a noise variation h^ξ. If D_ξu_t were exactly equal to D_{h^ξ}u_t, Malliavin integration by parts would bound D_ξP_tφ by ||φ||∞ times the cost of h^ξ. In infinite dimensions exact cancellation is generally impossible, so the paper chooses h^ξ to cancel the unstable components and leaves an error ρ_t; dissipativity makes ρ_t decay, producing the second term involving ||Dφ||.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For full space-time white noise, the cancellation problem may be easier than in the paper's finite-dimensional-noise setting because noise acts in infinitely many directions. For singular drift, this method suggests working first with smooth approximations and seeking estimates stable as the drift converges to δ_0.

proof_techniques.name: Regularized inverse of the Malliavin covariance
proof_techniques.description: The paper avoids needing a true inverse of the infinite-dimensional Malliavin covariance M_t. It uses (M_t+β)^{-1} and controls the defect R=β(M_t+β)^{-1}. A deterministic cone lemma shows that if M_t is bounded below on vectors with a significant projection onto a finite-dimensional subspace ΠH, then ΠR is small when β is small relative to that lower bound. This lets the proof cancel unstable or low-dimensional components without characterizing the full range of M_t.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Even if the singular SPDE lacks a classical global Malliavin inverse, it may suffice to prove lower bounds on finite-dimensional projections or low modes and combine them with heat-kernel contraction of high modes.

proof_techniques.name: High-mode contraction plus low-mode probabilistic smoothing
proof_techniques.description: The main theorem decomposes the tangent dynamics into a finite-dimensional subspace ΠH and its complement. The complement is made contractive by the parabolic semigroup, while the ΠH component is controlled probabilistically through the Malliavin covariance. This is a standard infinite-dimensional replacement for uniform ellipticity: only finitely many unstable directions must be actively smoothed, because high modes are damped.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a stochastic heat equation on [0,1], the Dirichlet Laplacian gives strong high-mode damping. One can try to prove low-mode smoothing from the additive space-time noise and use this decomposition to establish ASF or strong Feller.

proof_techniques.name: Backward adjoint representation of the Malliavin matrix
proof_techniques.description: The paper represents <M_tϕ,ϕ> as Σ_k∫_0^t <g_k,K_{s,t}ϕ>^2 ds, where K is the backward adjoint linearization. This turns Malliavin nondegeneracy into a statement about whether a backward solution can remain nearly orthogonal to all forced directions. It is the basis for the Hörmander bracket induction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For additive noise diagonalized in the Dirichlet eigenbasis, a similar representation can identify which modes are directly forced and how the linearized singular drift affects backward observability.

proof_techniques.name: Hörmander bracket induction in infinite dimensions
proof_techniques.description: For finite-dimensional degenerate noise, the paper proves that small Malliavin covariance implies smallness of progressively larger Lie-bracket families. It uses the polynomial structure of the drift, expansion in Brownian monomials, and time differentiation of pairings with the backward adjoint. Once enough brackets span the desired finite-dimensional projection, the covariance has inverse moments on that projection.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original equation has non-polynomial distributional drift and infinite-dimensional noise, so this bracket machinery is not directly applicable. However, for smooth approximations of the drift it suggests a way to prove finite-dimensional density or smoothing estimates if direct ellipticity estimates are unavailable.

proof_techniques.name: Wiener-polynomial replacement for Norris' lemma
proof_techniques.description: Because the backward adjoint processes are non-adapted, classical Norris lemma cannot be applied. The paper proves that a polynomial in Brownian motions with sufficiently regular, possibly non-adapted coefficients cannot be uniformly small unless all coefficients are small or some coefficient oscillates very rapidly. A priori time-regularity estimates rule out the rapid-oscillation alternative.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof for the regularized singular SPDE leads to non-adapted Brownian polynomial expressions, this theorem provides a template for extracting coefficient smallness without adapted semimartingale decompositions.

proof_techniques.name: Deterministic control to force support overlap
proof_techniques.description: In the Ginzburg-Landau example, the paper proves accessibility by constructing explicit deterministic controls in the Cameron-Martin directions and then invoking support of Brownian paths. The controlled PDE is driven close to a carefully chosen target, and dissipativity makes that target uniformly bounded. This verifies the topological hypothesis needed for uniqueness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an additive-noise stochastic heat equation, support of the noise is often rich; one may try to show that deterministic controls can steer any initial condition into a common neighborhood, perhaps near zero or a fixed smooth profile, then apply Corollary 1.4 or 2.2.

proof_techniques.name: Malliavin smoothness of finite-dimensional projections
proof_techniques.description: The paper combines inverse-moment bounds for projected Malliavin matrices with higher variation estimates to obtain C^∞ densities of finite-dimensional projections of transition probabilities. The higher variations are controlled recursively by parabolic smoothing and polynomial bounds on the nonlinearity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE or its smooth approximations, finite-dimensional densities can support irreducibility or support-overlap arguments. Smoothness of finite-dimensional projections may be easier to prove than full strong Feller for the singular limiting equation.

other_useful_info: The paper repeatedly emphasizes that asymptotic strong Feller is designed for infinite-dimensional systems where total-variation strong Feller or minorization may fail. It also stresses that accessibility or controllability is often a separate, model-specific problem; the smoothing theorem alone does not prove uniqueness without a support-overlap argument. The detailed framework assumes finite-dimensional additive noise and polynomial nonlinearities, so it does not directly cover a stochastic heat equation driven by space-time white noise with distributional drift; nevertheless the abstract ergodic criteria in Sections 1 and 2 are independent of those structural assumptions. Relevant cited sources within the paper include HM06 for asymptotic strong Feller theory, HM08/HMS10 for Wasserstein spectral gaps, DPZ96 and DPEZ95 for strong Feller methods in semilinear SPDEs, and Cerrai 1999 for ergodicity of stochastic reaction-diffusion systems with polynomial coefficients.
why_relevant: This develops Malliavin/asymptotic-strong-Feller methods for semilinear parabolic SPDEs with additive noise. Its framework gives a possible route to smoothing estimates and uniqueness of invariant measures for the approximating equations and possibly their singular limit.
